返回本批次

许多非洲家庭花费巨资埋葬死者

Many African families spend fortunes burying their dead

davidoks.blog 作者 powera 分数 146 评论 119 2026/04/10 06:10
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 考虑一下加纳的葬礼。假设您是一位年长的加纳人，假设您是科菲，74 岁，阿肯人。有一天，你做了所有人类都必须做的事，然后死去。也许您死在加纳首都阿克拉北郊简陋的平房里。接下来会发生什么？有几件事。首先，你的直系亲属会发现你死了；而且，为了处理你死亡后的后勤事宜，他们会打电话给你大家庭的家长：abusuapanyin。这不是你幸存的最亲近的亲戚。阿坎人是母系氏族，母系“拥有”身体，所以阿布苏阿帕因是母亲这边最年长的男性。从现在开始，他就是负责安排的人。与他协商后，您的尸体将被送往最近的医院太平间，在那里进行防腐处理并放入冷藏室中。您的尸体将在该冷藏室中保存很长时间。通常需要数周或数月；有时尸体可以冷藏一整年。为什么这么久？因为尸体在太平间停留的时间越长，家人就越有时间筹集资金来举办真正适合您身份的葬礼。而且，由于医院对你的尸体存放的时间每增加一周，收取的费用就会不断增加，因此将你的尸体长期冷藏本身就是一种声望的标志。最终，你的家人决定他们已经筹集了他们要筹集的资金。因此，他们选择了一个星期六——加纳基督教徒的葬礼总是在星期六举行——并计划了一场盛大的活动，事实上，这个活动将持续三天。他们聘请平面设计师制作大型彩色横幅，上面印有您的姓名、照片、出生日期和死亡日期以及葬礼的时间和地点：这些横幅挂在城市各处十字路口的墙壁和栅栏上。他们租一个场地，雇用一大批员工——餐饮承办人、DJ 或现场乐队、摄影师，也许还有摄像师——并为家人选择葬礼布料。如果你的家人能负担得起，或者希望社区相信他们能负担得起，他们就会委托一位工匠为你雕刻一个“梦幻棺材”，形状就像你在生活中喜欢或欣赏的东西：也许是可可豆荚、一栋校舍、一只螃蟹、一支画笔或一个巨大的蓝色茶壶。最后，在这一切之后，重要的一天到来了。你的尸体从太平间被运出；数百人出现，其中许多人一生中从未认识过您；你会注意到，这一切听起来都非常昂贵。事实确实如此。在加纳举办一场中等规模的葬礼大约需要 5,000 美元；一件“合适的”很容易花费 15,000 或 20,000 美元。而这一切都发生在一个年收入中位数约为 1,500 美元的国家。加纳以其特别华丽的葬礼文化而闻名。但这并不是撒哈拉以南非洲唯一一个拥有昂贵葬礼文化的地方。例如，南非东部夸祖鲁纳塔尔省的普通家庭在一次葬礼上的花费相当于一个成年人的年收入。我们在很多地方都看到了同样的超昂贵葬礼趋势：刚果民主共和国、肯尼亚、尼日利亚、贝宁、乌干达、喀麦隆、莫桑比克、科特迪瓦。事实上，人们经常观察到，家庭在埋葬死者上的花费比在让病人活下去上花费的钱还要多：事实上，在坦桑尼亚北部的卡盖拉地区，家庭在葬礼上的花费比在医疗保健上的花费多出 50%。那么人们如何支付这些非常昂贵的事件呢？有时他们会购买某种保险：葬礼保险是撒哈拉以南地区最受欢迎的金融产品之一。事实上，非洲往往比健康保险更受欢迎。大多数时候，家庭成员会用别人的贷款来支付葬礼费用。例如，夸祖鲁-纳塔尔省大约四分之一的家庭通过负债来支付葬礼费用。而且，如果到了这种情况，家庭只会削减生活开支来支付葬礼费用。在津巴布韦，无力埋葬死者的家庭会变卖财产，甚至削减食物。 （津巴布韦的营养不良死亡率位居世界第二。）这实际上是一件非常普遍的事情。在肯尼亚西部 25 年内陷入贫困的 325 个家庭中，63% 的家庭将“与葬礼相关的巨额开支”视为主要原因。这似乎不是什么新鲜事。早在 1853 年，一位到访西非沿海的游客就指出，“按照国家的观念，即使是最贫穷的人也会典当和奴役自己，以获得体面地埋葬亲人的手段。”这一切似乎都很奇怪。我们这些生活在富裕国家的人往往期望支出遵循某种马斯洛夫式的进展。穷人把钱花在必需品上；随着人们变得更加富有，他们他们把钱花在越来越远离生存逻辑的事情上，直到最终他们愿意将资源分配到像狗的长寿这样的事情上。但随着非洲葬礼支出的增加，这种模式发生了逆转。为什么世界上一些最贫穷的人会倾家荡产来支付极其奢华的葬礼？对此问题的标准答案是，过度的葬礼支出是“当地文化的一部分”，尤其反映了“对长辈的尊敬”。确实如此。但这只会引出一个问题：为什么它是文化的一部分——事实上，为什么它是非洲众多不同文化的一部分。为什么繁重的葬礼开支成为生活中如此重要的一部分？如果这仅仅是“尊敬长辈”的体现，为什么这么多非洲老人抱怨他们的葬礼比他们生前的照顾要多得多？ （这种情绪如此普遍，以至于阿坎人有句俗话：abusua do funu，“家人喜欢尸体。”）我认为，答案是葬礼并不是真正关于死者的。葬礼是亲属群体忠诚度的昂贵信号：在这种情况下，葬礼的费用才是重点。反过来，葬礼告诉我们很多关于为什么非洲各地如此多的社会在实现经济“起飞”方面遇到如此多的困难。血缘社会对经济增长充满敌意，因为经济增长破坏了血缘关系的基础：这就是为什么血缘社会要求持续的、明显的财富牺牲——葬礼是最壮观的——这使得任何个人都很难积累资本、再投资他们的资产并取得进步。葬礼是了解财富毁灭体系的一个窗口，该体系首先使人们陷入贫困。非洲社会作为一个广泛的模式，有着异常紧密的亲属关系。只有少数其他地方——阿富汗南部的普什图族腹地、车臣和达吉斯坦的山区、新几内亚的丛林——表现出与撒哈拉以南非洲大部分地区相当的亲属关系强度。这并不是整个非洲的普遍模式——例如喀拉哈里沙漠的桑人，他们的社会安排相对灵活——但总的趋势是明确的：非洲社会总体上是亲属关系社会。那么什么是亲属关系社会？你可以把现代社会想象成大量个体的集合，他们的生活由国家和公司等非人格化机构构成。亲属社会要古老得多：事实上，它们是人类社会最古老、最持久的类型。在亲属关系社会中，生活以大家庭为中心：“氏族”、血统、部落——这个群体通常包括许多实际上没有血缘关系的人。这些亲属网络的行为与现代社会中的核心家庭完全不同。它们是功能强大的有机体：现代世界中国家提供的大部分功能——保护免受伤害、信用、争端解决、老年护理、社会保险——都是由亲属关系网络提供的。如果你生病了，亲属团体会照顾你；如果你需要现金，亲属团体会借钱给你；如果一个陌生人得罪了你，亲属团体就会为你报仇。 当然，亲属网络不是慈善机构。这更像是一个你生来就无法离开的互助社会：亲属群体给予的，亲属群体也必须接受。亲属社会中的大量生活是由人们对亲属的义务构成的。最极端的例子是为亲属群体而战斗和杀戮的义务：因此，从阿巴拉契亚到索马里草原，血仇和仇杀是强烈的亲属导向社会的特征。但在更日常的层面上，你也欠你的亲属网络一些东西：我们可以称之为分享义务。正如你向富裕世界管理生活的各种非个人机构缴纳税款和费用一样，你也必须定期为你的亲属的集体福利做出贡献。但有一个关键的区别。在现代社会，你或多或少会知道自己欠了什么，什么时候欠；但对于分担义务，就没有这样的明确性了。你亲戚的要求——医院账单、贷款申请、丧葬费用——就这么出现了。而且你真的不能拒绝这些义务。定义紧密亲属关系的相互义务对于亲属社会日常生活的运作确实至关重要。未能表现出对团体忠诚度的人可能会失去团体提供的一切。这种威胁在传统文化中得到了强有力的体现。在一个你在亲属关系网络中的地位往往是你最重要的事情的社会里，被抛弃就是一种社会死亡。所以，在一个亲属关系社会里，你赚到的东西都不是真正属于你的。如果你赚到的钱超出了维持生计的程度，你就会被期望与他人分享你不幸的亲戚；如果你创办一家企业，你将被期望雇用你的表兄弟、侄子或姻亲，即使他们不是最好的员工；如果你买了一辆车，你就会被期望把它借给需要它的亲戚。其结果是不断地从亲属群体中生产力最高的成员向生产力最低的成员进行重新分配的过程。这种非正式的再分配是非洲社会生活的一个持续特征：93％的肯尼亚企业家认为商业上的成功会导致家人和朋友的财务需求。南非人甚至为定义非洲亲属群体的分担义务起了一个名字：“黑人税”。当然，这对于社会中雄心勃勃、富有成效的人来说是一笔糟糕的交易。但由于拒绝是不可能的，分担义务会导致各种混淆视听的尝试。一项实验发现，肯尼亚农村妇女愿意支付大笔费用，以便向亲戚隐瞒自己的收入；同样，在喀麦隆，人们通常会假装比实际情况更穷，从而避免分担义务，通过用自己的储蓄全额抵押来提取不必要的银行贷款。有时，人们通过远离家乡工作来逃避分担义务。正如内罗毕的一位女商人所证明的那样：我在远离家乡的情况下在无人知晓的情况下出售二手衣服。我躲着我的朋友，因为我相信不是所有的朋友都会为我的成功而高兴，而我却从家人那里营造出我没有钱的画面，让他们为了自己的钱而努力工作。我以前的生意是一家街边餐馆，由于我的姻亲利用我的钱而失败了，但我想扩大它。无情的分担义务也使得随着时间的推移几乎不可能积累储蓄。因此，我们看到，在夸祖鲁-纳塔尔省，个人会不遗余力地将剩余资金投资于不可共享的商品，如屋顶或栅栏，而不是积累家人可能要求的流动储蓄。当然，这种类型的混淆对于亲属关系网络来说是一个重大问题。如果团体中富有成效的成员可以背叛——从公共池中夺走他们的资源——那么整个相互义务体系就会开始瓦解。如果一个富有成效的个人可以简单地退出分担义务，那么网络必须对留下来的人提出更多要求，从而增加背叛的动机：因此整个微妙的相互义务机制会慢慢崩溃。这就是亲属关系网络的死亡螺旋。所以从亲属关系网络的角度来看，财富是一种威胁。那些变得富有的人有叛逃的动机；虽然社会制裁可以惩罚那些公开叛逃的人，但监管那些悄悄叛逃的人（比如内罗毕的女商人）却要困难得多。最安全的选择是从一开始就防止人们变得太富有。因此，随着时间的推移，以紧密亲属关系为基础的社会已经制定了使叛逃变得困难的策略。其中最重要的是对个人财富的仪式性破坏。这就是为什么亲属社会似乎有如此多的仪式——公共宴会、精心赠送的礼物、夸富宴——在外人看来是如此奇怪和浪费。你所承担的费用是你无法超越亲属关系的一种保证。你可以将葬礼视为另一种财富毁灭仪式。它的天才之处在于它是无法回避的：这是一场几乎致力于牺牲财富的公共仪式。私下里，您也许可以通过隐藏您的收入或储蓄来逃避分享义务；但在公开场合，在葬礼上，你的亲属对你的财富的主张是最明显、最难以避免的。你不能简单地不参加你叔叔的葬礼；而且，如果你出现，显然会被期望贡献一笔可观的金额。而这种逻辑对于那些涉嫌逃避亲属义务的人来说更加有力。在葬礼上，你必须最大声地表明你愿意履行分享义务。葬礼的奢华是一个代价高昂的信号，表明人们继续致力于将亲属群体团结在一起的相互义务制度。关键是它很昂贵，而且与你的经济能力不相称。这就是为什么加纳的葬礼随着时间的推移往往变得更加奢华。过去，亲属群体的大多数成员生活在相对较小的半径内，尸体的分解自然限制了能够及时调集用于仪式的资源；于是几天之内尸体就被埋葬了，而那些不能来的人稍后会得到“二葬”。 （在尼日利亚东部的伊博兰和喀麦隆农村的大部分地区，仍然实行第二次埋葬。）但在二十世纪下半叶，这种动态发生了变化。移民数量大幅增加，无论是前往主要城市或其他国家，飞机使人们可以在国外工作并定期回国；冷藏可以使尸体保存更长时间。因此，对丧葬费用的旧有限制被打破了。移民普遍被怀疑逃避亲属义务，因此渴望表现出他们对亲属群体的持续忠诚：而葬礼，可以让这种忠诚变得有形和公开，是做到这一点的完美场所。因此，在20世纪下半叶，加纳出现了巨大的葬礼经济。尸体可以无限期地冷藏在医院的太平间里，而且由于费用每周都在上涨，因此长期冷藏尸体变得享有盛誉。到了 2000 年代，许多加纳医院通过存放尸体获得的收入比治疗活体病人的收入还要多。我认为，葬礼仪式有些相当病态。紧密的亲属关系义务使人们很难积累储蓄、对自己的企业进行再投资或做出最有利于自己和子女的财务决策。每一份盈余都在复合之前被索取。亲属关系网络确实提供了许多重要的福利功能：人们希望看到他们的亲戚有饭吃、有住处、有照顾，而亲属关系网络就是实现这一目标的机制之一。但很难不注意到更暗的东西。亲属关系网络对阻止其任何成员变得足够繁荣而不再需要它有着强烈的兴趣：不再需要你帮助的人也可能不会帮助你。因此，明显的动机是阻碍最有生产力的人：亲属关系团体对其最有生产力的成员提出的要求不仅仅是团结的要求，而是对一种强制平庸的要求。人们遵守这些要求不仅是出于真正的忠诚，而且还因为担心拒绝会发生什么。你会注意到，这种动态与持久的经济增长并不真正兼容。在血缘文化密集的地方，经济发展异常困难。在很大程度上，这是因为亲属关系的忠诚排挤了对非个人机构的忠诚：例如，缺乏非个人的社会信任就是非洲社会很少有大企业的原因。但这也是因为将亲属关系社会凝聚在一起的粘合剂是偶尔牺牲积累的财富。几乎每个经济上成功的社会都从强调亲属要求的社会秩序转变为强调个人权利的社会秩序，这是有原因的。生活在一个由非个人制度统治的个人社会中，我们对想象中的温暖社区和深厚家庭纽带的世界怀有一种可以理解的渴望。但我们忘记了这个社会世界是多么令人窒息，它对最具生产力的成员是多么寄生，以及它对经济发展的前景是多么有害。对强国的忠诚不会很快取代对亲属网络的忠诚。但对于被困在这些亲属关系网络中的最有生产力的人来说，我确实认为技术提供了类似于逃生舱的东西。以单一名字持有的手机和银行账户是帮助这些人在他们的收入和家人知道他们的收入之间建立隔离墙的工具。在许多情况下，这些技术具有显着的解放性。能够获得隐性收入的塞内加尔妇女立即将向亲戚的转移减少了四分之一，并将这些钱用于自己的医疗保健。那么，对于现代金融体系中最被低估的美德之一：隐私，有很多话要说。归根结底，社会现代性实际上就是不必按照家人的吩咐去做——嫁给你想要的人，做你想要的工作，按照你想要的方式花钱。当然，这其中有一些冷酷的东西，但也有一些深刻的解放。在一个你的亲戚可以看到并声称拥有你所赚的一切的世界里，任何让你的收入对他们来说不那么清晰的东西也悄然成为经济发展的引擎。因此，归根结底，奢华的葬礼并不是非洲生活中奇怪的文化怪癖，而是以破坏积累的剩余为导向的社会秩序的最明显表现。在这种社会秩序的控制放松之前，非洲生产的大部分财富将继续流向地下。

Original Summary

  1. Consider, for a moment, a funeral in Ghana.Suppose you’re an elderly Ghanaian—let’s say you’re Kofi, age 74, an Akan. One day, you do as all humans must and die. Perhaps you die at your modest bungalow in a northern suburb of Accra, Ghana’s capital. What happens next?A few things. First, your immediate family will discover that you’re dead; and, in order to deal with the logistics of your death, they call the head of your extended family: the abusuapanyin. This is not your closest surviving relative. The Akan are matrilineal, and the maternal line “owns” the body, so the abusuapanyin is the most senior male on your mother’s side. And he’s the one who will take charge of the arrangements from this point forward. In consultation with him, your body is taken to the nearest hospital mortuary, where it’s embalmed and placed in a refrigerated unit.Your body is going to remain in that refrigerated unit for a long time. Typically it will be weeks or months; sometimes bodies can stay refrigerated for an entire year. Why so long? Because the longer that the body stays in the mortuary, the more time the family has to raise funds for a funeral truly befitting your status. And, since the hospital charges escalating fees for each additional week that your body is stored there, keeping your body refrigerated for a long time is itself a mark of prestige.Eventually, your family decides that they’ve raised the funds they’re going to raise. So they pick a Saturday—the funerals of Christian Ghanaians are always held on Saturdays—and plan a lavish event that will, in fact, stretch across three days. They hire a graphic designer to produce large colorful banners bearing your name, your photograph, your dates of birth and death, and the time and place of your funeral: these are hung on walls and fences at intersections around the city. They rent a venue, hire a large staff—caterers, a DJ or live band, a photographer, maybe also a videographer—and choose a funeral cloth for the family to wear. And if your family can afford it, or wants the community to believe that they can, they commission a craftsman to carve you a “fantasy coffin” shaped like something you enjoyed or admired in life: perhaps a cocoa pod, a school building, a crab, a paintbrush, or a giant blue teapot.And, finally, after all this, the big day comes. Your body is retrieved from the mortuary; hundreds of people show up, many of whom never knew you in life; and a great deal of money is spent feeding them, entertaining them, and sending you off in the style that an Akan elder deserves.This all sounds, you’ll notice, very expensive. And it is.A modest, mid-level funeral in Ghana costs about $5,000 U.S. dollars; a “befitting” one can easily cost $15,000 or $20,000. And all this in a country with a median income of about $1,500 per year. Ghana is known for its particularly ornate funeral culture; but it’s not the only place in sub-Saharan Africa with a culture of exorbitantly expensive funerals. The average household in KwaZulu-Natal in eastern South Africa, for example, spends the equivalent of an adult’s annual income on a single funeral. We see the same tendency for ultra-expensive funerals in a striking number of places: the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Kenya, Nigeria, Benin, Uganda, Cameroon, Mozambique, the Ivory Coast. It’s often observed, in fact, that families will spend more money on burying the dead than on keeping the sick alive: indeed, in the Kagera region of northern Tanzania, families spend 50 percent more money on funerals than on medical care.So how do people pay for these remarkably expensive events?Sometimes they’ll have insurance of some kind: funeral insurance, where the payout is earmarked for the funeral costs, is one of the most popular financial products in sub-Saharan Africa—often, in fact, more popular than health insurance. And much of the time, family members will pay for funerals with loans from others. About a quarter of households in KwaZulu-Natal, for example, pay for funerals by going into debt.And, if it comes to it, families will just cut back on living expenses to cover the funeral costs. In Zimbabwe, households that cannot afford to bury their dead will sell their belongings or even cut back on food. (Zimbabwe has the second-highest rate of death from malnutrition in the world.) This is actually a remarkably common thing. Out of 325 families that declined into poverty in western Kenya over a period of 25 years, 63 percent cited “heavy expenses related to funerals” as a major cause. And this doesn’t seem to be a new thing. As early as 1853, a visitor to coastal West Africa noted that “even the poorest will pawn and enslave themselves to obtain the means of burying a relation decently, according to the ideas of country.”This all seems very strange.Those of us who live in the rich world tend to expect that spending follows a sort of Maslovian progression. Poor people spend money on the necessities; as people get richer, they spend on things further and further removed from the logic of survival, until eventually they are willing to allocate resources to things like dog longevity. But with African funeral spending, this pattern is inverted. Why do some of the poorest people in the world bankrupt themselves to pay for extremely lavish funerals?The standard answer to this is that exorbitant funeral spending is “part of the local culture,” and particularly reflective of a “reverence toward elders.” And indeed that is true. But that only begs the question of why it’s part of the culture—in fact, why it’s part of so many different cultures across Africa. Why is heavy funeral spending such a pronounced part of life? And if this is merely a reflection of “reverence toward elders,” why do so many elderly Africans complain that far more attention is given to their funeral than to their care while alive? (So common is this sentiment that the Akan have a saying: abusua do funu, “the family loves the corpse.”)The answer, I think, is that the funeral isn’t really about the deceased. Funerals function as a costly signal of kinship group loyalty: and in that context, the expense of the funeral is the point. And, in turn, funerals tell us quite a lot about why so many societies across Africa have had so much trouble achieving economic “takeoff.” Kinship societies are actively hostile to economic growth, because economic growth undermines the basis of kinship: that is why kinship societies demand constant, visible sacrifices of wealth—funerals being the most spectacular—that make it extraordinarily difficult for any individual to accumulate capital, reinvest their assets, and pull ahead. The funeral is a window into a system of wealth destruction that serves, above all else, to keep people poor.African societies, as a broad pattern, have extraordinarily intense kinship ties. Only a few other places—the Pashtun heartlands of southern Afghanistan, the mountains of Chechnya and Dagestan, the jungles of New Guinea—exhibit kinship intensity on par with what prevails in much of sub-Saharan Africa. This is not a universal pattern across all of Africa—the San people of the Kalahari desert, for example, have relatively flexible social arrangements—but the general tendency is clear: African societies, by and large, are kinship societies.So what are kinship societies?You can think of modern societies as large collections of individuals, their lives structured by impersonal institutions like states and corporations. Kinship societies are much older: they are, in fact, the oldest and most durable type of human society. In a kinship society, life is centered on the extended family: the “clan,” the lineage, the tribe—a group that often includes many people who aren’t actually related. These kinship networks don’t act anything like nuclear families in modern societies. They are highly functional organisms: most of the functions provided by states in the modern world—protection from harm, credit, dispute resolution, eldercare, social insurance—are instead provided by the kinship network. If you fall sick, the kinship group will care for you; if you need cash, the kinship group will lend you money; if a stranger wrongs you, the kinship group will avenge you.Of course, a kinship network isn’t a charity. It’s more like a mutual aid society that you’re born into and can’t leave: what the kinship group gives the kinship group must also take. A huge amount of life in kinship societies is structured by the obligations that people owe to their kin.The most extreme example of this is the obligation to fight and kill for your kin group if it comes to it: thus the blood feuds and vendettas that characterize intensely kin-oriented societies, from Appalachia to the Somali steppe.But you also owe things to your kinship network on a more day-to-day level: we can call these sharing obligations. Just as you pay taxes and fees to the various impersonal institutions that govern life in the rich world, you must make regular contributions to the collective welfare of your kin. But there’s a crucial distinction. In a modern society, you will know, more or less, what you owe and when you’ll owe it; but with sharing obligations there’s no such clarity. The demands from your kin—hospital bills, loan requests, funeral expenses—simply come up.And you can’t really say no to these obligations. The mutual obligation that defines intensive kinship really is essential to the functioning of everyday life in kinship societies. A person who fails to demonstrate loyalty to the group risks losing access to everything the group provides. And this threat is powerfully enforced in traditional cultures. In a society where your standing in the kinship network is often the single most important thing about you, being cast out is a kind of social death.And so, in a kinship society, nothing that you earn is truly yours. If you make money beyond the point of subsistence, you’ll be expected to share it with your less-fortunate relatives; if you start a business, you’ll be expected to hire your cousins or nephews or in-laws, even if they’re not the best possible employees; if you buy a car, you’ll be expected to lend it out to relatives who need it. The result is a constant process of redistribution from the most productive members of a kinship group to the least productive. This informal redistribution is a constant feature of life in African societies: 93 percent of Kenyan entrepreneurs agree that success in business leads to financial demands from family and friends. South Africans even have a name for the sharing obligations that define African kinship groups: “the black tax.”This is, of course, a bad deal for the ambitious and productive within the society. But because refusal is impossible, sharing obligations lead to all sorts of attempts at obfuscation. One experiment found that rural Kenyan women were willing to pay significant sums in order to hide their income from relatives; likewise, in Cameroon, it’s common for people to pretend to be poorer than they are, and thus avoid sharing obligations, by taking out unnecessary bank loans fully collateralized by their savings. Sometimes people get around sharing obligations by working far from home. As one businesswoman in Nairobi attested:I sell second-hand clothes without anyone knowing, far from home. I hide from my friends because I believe not all friends will be happy with my success, and from family to create a picture that I have no money, for them to work hard for their own money. My previous business, a street-side restaurant, failed due to my in-laws using me for money, yet I wanted to expand it.The relentlessness of sharing obligations also makes it nearly impossible to accrue savings over time. Thus we see that in KwaZulu-Natal individuals will go out of their way to invest their surplus in non-sharable goods, like roofing or fencing, instead of accumulating liquid savings that their families might claim.Of course, this type of obfuscation is a significant problem for the kinship network. If the productive members of the group can defect—removing their resources from the common pool—then the whole system of mutual obligation begins to unravel. If a productive individual can simply withdraw from sharing obligations, then the network must demand more from those who remain, increasing the incentive to defect: so the entire delicate machinery of mutual obligation collapses in a slow cascade. This is the death spiral for kinship networks.So from the perspective of the kinship network, wealth is a threat. Those who become wealthy have an incentive to defect; and while social sanctions can punish those who defect explicitly, it’s much harder to police those—like the businesswoman in Nairobi—who defect quietly. The safest bet is to prevent people from becoming too rich in the first place.So, over time, societies based around intensive kinship have developed strategies to make defection difficult. The most important of these is the ritualistic destruction of individual wealth. This is why kinship societies seem to have so many rituals—public feasting, elaborate gift-giving, potlatch—that seem so strange and wasteful to the outside observer. The expense that you incur is a sort of guarantee that you won’t be able to transcend the bonds of kinship.You can think of funerals as another wealth destruction ritual. The genius of it is that it can’t be evaded: it is a public ceremony virtually dedicated to the immolation of wealth. In private, you might be able to evade your sharing obligations by hiding your earnings or your savings; but in public, at the funeral, the claims that your kin make on your wealth are at their most visible and least avoidable. You can’t simply not show up to your uncle’s funeral; and, if you show up, you will obviously be expected to contribute a handsome sum.And this logic is even more powerful for those who are suspected of shirking their kinship obligations. It’s at the funeral where you must signal your willingness to honor sharing obligations most loudly. The lavishness of the funeral is a costly signal of continued commitment to the system of mutual obligation that holds the kinship group together. The point is that it’s expensive and incommensurate with your means.This is why Ghanaian funerals, for example, have tended to grow only more lavish with time. In the past, most members of a kinship group lived within a relatively small radius, and the decomposition of the body placed a natural limit on the resources that could be marshaled in time for the ceremony; so the body was buried within a few days, and those who couldn’t make it would be accommodated with a “second burial” later on. (The second burial is still practiced in places like Igboland in eastern Nigeria and in large parts of rural Cameroon.)But in the second half of the twentieth century, this dynamic changed. There was a huge increase in migration, either to major cities or to other countries, and airplanes made it possible for people to work abroad and return home on a regular basis; and refrigeration allowed bodies to be preserved for much longer. And so the old limitations on funeral expenses were blown open. Migrants were generally suspected of shirking their kinship obligations, and so were eager to demonstrate their continued loyalty to the kinship group: and the funeral, where this loyalty could be made tangible and public, was the perfect place to do it.And so in the second half of the twentieth century a huge funeral economy emerged in Ghana. Bodies could be refrigerated indefinitely in hospital mortuaries, and since the fees escalated with each passing week it became prestigious to refrigerate bodies for a long time; by the 2000s, many Ghanaian hospitals were earning more from storing dead bodies than from treating living patients.There is, I think, something quite pathological about the funeral ritual. The obligations of intensive kinship make it extraordinarily difficult for people to build savings, reinvest in their businesses, or make the financial decisions that would most benefit themselves and their children. Every surplus is claimed before it can compound.Kinship networks certainly do provide a number of important welfare functions: people want to see their relatives fed and housed and cared for, and a kinship network is, among other things, a mechanism for accomplishing that. But it’s hard not to notice something darker. The kinship network has a strong interest in preventing any of its members from becoming prosperous enough to no longer need it: someone who no longer needs your help is also someone who might not help you.The obvious incentive, then, is to hobble the most productive: the demands that the kinship group makes on its most productive members are not simply demands for solidarity but demands for a kind of enforced mediocrity. People comply with these demands not only out of genuine loyalty but also out of fear of what happens if they refuse.This dynamic, you’ll notice, isn’t really compatible with durable economic growth. Economic development is extraordinarily difficult in intensive kinship culture. In large part, this is because kinship loyalties crowd out loyalties to impersonal institutions: this lack of impersonal social trust is why African societies have so few large firms, for example. But it’s also because the glue that holds together kinship society is the occasional immolation of built-up wealth.There’s a reason why virtually every economically successful society has graduated from a social order that stresses the claims of kin into one that stresses the rights of individuals. Living in a society of individuals governed by impersonal institutions, we have an understandable wistfulness for the imagined world of warm communities and thick familial bonds. But we forget how suffocating that social world is, how parasitical it is on its most productive members, and how poisonous it is for any prospect of economic development.I don’t think that African societies are ripe for social transformations of the kind just described; loyalties to strong states won’t supplant loyalties to kinship networks anytime soon. But for the most productive people trapped inside these kinship networks, I do think that technology offers something like an escape hatch. Mobile phones and bank accounts held under a single name are tools that help these people put a wall between what they earn and what their family knows they earn. In many cases these technologies are remarkably liberating. Senegalese women who were able to receive hidden income immediately cut transfers to relatives by a quarter and spent the money on healthcare for themselves.There’s a lot to be said, then, for one of the most underappreciated virtues of modern financial systems: privacy. Social modernity, in the end, is really about not having to do what your family tells you to do—marrying whom you want, taking the job you want, and spending your earnings the way you want. There is something cold about this, of course, but also something deeply emancipating. In a world where your relatives can see and lay claim to everything you earn, anything that makes your income a little less legible to them is also, quietly, an engine of economic development.And so the lavish funeral, in the end, is not a strange cultural quirk of African life, but the most visible manifestation of a social order oriented toward the destruction of accumulated surplus. And until the grip of that social order loosens, much of the wealth that Africa produces will continue to go, quite literally, into the ground.No posts

评论（默认中文）

kenferry 2026/04/10 07:50
关于丧葬费用的事实材料很有趣，但是当它进入“亲属社会是破坏财富的社会”时，它似乎相当……不受支持？这是一个笼统的说法，实际上需要了解整个情况，而整个情况并没有被呈现出来。是否有理由认为作者确实拥有做出这些主张的所有背景？
The factual material about funeral spending costs is very interesting, but when it gets into "Kinship societies are wealth-destroying societies" it seems rather… unsupported? That's a sweeping statement that actually requires understanding the whole picture, and the whole picture is not being presented. Is there reason to think the author truly has all the context to make these claims?
在 HN 中查看
tyeaglet 2026/04/10 10:47
韩国曾经也有过类似的现象，不过不是为了葬礼。当最年长的男人（可能是一个大家庭的祖父）度过他的 60 岁生日时，全家人都必须举办为期几天的聚会来庆祝。这就像家庭其他成员的一项家庭责任，它深深地融入到文化中，所以他们不会简单地考虑拥有一个小孩子的替代方案。只有当聚会足够大时，当地社区的其他长辈才会说“干得好”。 盛大的庆祝活动结束后，家里的其他人将背负巨额债务，在可预见的未来，他们的收入都无法偿还。老人去世了，一家人在债务的痛苦中生活。以前一直是这样，直到韩国成为工业国家，越来越多的人开始活到60岁以上。 我妈妈仍然在谈论以前的样子。
Korea used to have something similar to this phenomenon, although it wasn’t for the funeral. When the oldest man (probably the grandfather of a big family) has his 60th birthday, the entire family had to celebrate with basically throwing a days-long party. It was like a family duty for the rest of the family, and it was embedded into the culture so deeply so they wouldn’t simply think about the alternative of having a small one. Other elders in the local community would say “well done” only when the party was big enough. After the big celebration, the rest of the family would sit on a massive debt, which couldn’t be reimbursed with their earnings for a foreseeable future. The old man dies, and the family lives along with the agony of the debt. It used to be the case until Korea became an industrial country and a lot more people started having more than 60 yrs of life. My mom still talks about what it used to look like in those old days.
在 HN 中查看
marcus_holmes 2026/04/10 10:49
它是通过英国资本主义观点的视角来看待局势的。

我在柬埔寨工作时也发现了同样的事情；高棉文化非常非常家庭化，大家庭是高棉人主要的生存机制，个人意愿往往服从于家庭。这是他们的文化，高棉人对此感到满意，这就是他们选择的生活方式。盎格鲁侨民（包括我）不理解它，认为它是压迫性的，并且有一种自然的本能将高棉人民从这种压迫中“解放”出来。我花了很长时间与高棉人交谈，才意识到他们看待世界的方式与我截然不同，从这个角度来看，这一切都是有效的，并且是他们快乐和安慰的源泉。显然，有些人是局外人，这对他们不起作用，但盎格鲁文化也是如此。

It's viewing the situation through the lens of Anglo capitalist opinions.

I found the same thing when working in Cambodia; Khmer culture is very, very, family-oriented, the extended family is the main survival mechanism for Khmer people, and individual wishes are often subordinated to the family. This is their culture, Khmer people are happy with it, this is how they choose to live. The Anglo ex-pats (including me) don't understand it, find it oppressive and have a natural instinct to "liberate" Khmer people from this oppression. Took me quite a while of talking with Khmer people to realise that they look at the world very differently from me, and from that perspective this all works and is a source of joy and comfort for them. Obviously there are outliers and people who this doesn't work for, but that's also true of Anglo culture.

在 HN 中查看
decimalenough 2026/04/10 09:17
这并不是一个新奇的观察，例如卡普辛斯基的《在太阳的阴影下》描述了同样的现象：出人头地是非常困难的，因为任何高于赤裸生存的东西都会立即被你的亲属吸走。
This is not a novel observation, eg Kapuscinski's "In the Shadow of the Sun" describes the same phenomenon: it's very difficult to get ahead because anything above bare subsistence is immediately siphoned off by your kin.

marcus_holmes 2026/04/10 10:50
另一方面是，也很难落后太多。你的亲属也有义务赡养你。
The flip side is that it's very difficult to fall too far behind as well. Your kin have an obligation to support you, too.

在 HN 中查看
nostrademons 2026/04/10 09:53
事实上，亲属社会与经济逆风之间的关系已有相当多的记录[1][2]。 该机制与共产主义/社会主义社会经常失败的原因相同：当财富属于每个人时，没有人有动力或手段来积累财富，这阻碍了社会内部的资本形成[3]。

文章掩盖的部分是“亲属社会破坏经济增长”是“经济增长破坏家庭形成”的罗素共轭[4]。 亲属关系网络为一些重要的社区功能提供了重要的无形支持，特别是抚养孩子。 这就是“养育一个孩子需要一个村庄”的格言。 当你允许人们以积累财富的名义背叛他们的社会义务时，事实证明他们 做，村庄受苦。 正如文章所说：“亲属关系网络有强烈的兴趣，阻止其任何成员变得足够富裕而不再需要它：不再需要你帮助的人也可能不会帮助你。” 这正是我们在现代工业化经济体中所观察到的情况，人们变得越来越原子化，那些创造归属感和互助（更不用说团体儿童保育和社会化）等东西的非正式社区组织逐渐消亡，因为每个人都在追逐能让他们负担更高的机构儿童保育费用的晋升。

这就是为什么每个主要工业化国家的生育率都会急剧下降，通常是在工业化的同时。

[1] https://www.uni-heidelberg.de/md/awi/forschung/paper_e.bulte...

[2] https://edepot.wur.nl/14918

[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tragedy_of_the_commons

[4] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emotive_conjugation

On a factual level the relationship between kinship societies and economic headwinds is fairly well documented [1] [2]. The mechanism is the same reason that communist/socialist societies often fail: when wealth belongs to everyone, nobody has either the incentive or the means to accumulate wealth, which prevents capital formation within the society [3].

The part that the article glosses over is that "Kinship societies destroy economic growth" is a Russell conjugate [4] of "economic growth destroys family formation". Kinship networks provide important intangible support to several important community functions, notably child-rearing. That's the whole "it takes a village to raise a child" aphorism. When you allow people to defect on their social obligations in the name of accumulating wealth, then it turns out they do, and the village suffers. It is exactly as the article said: "The kinship network has a strong interest in preventing any of its members from becoming prosperous enough to no longer need it: someone who no longer needs your help is also someone who might not help you." That's exactly what we've observed happening in modern industrialized economies, where people become increasingly atomized and those informal community organizations that create things like belonging and mutual aid (not to mention group childcare and socialization) die off as everyone chases the promotion that will let them afford ever-higher institutional childcare costs.

And this is why the fertility rate in every major industrialized country has cratered, usually right as it industrializes.

[1] https://www.uni-heidelberg.de/md/awi/forschung/paper_e.bulte...

[2] https://edepot.wur.nl/14918

[3] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tragedy_of_the_commons

[4] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emotive_conjugation

levocardia 2026/04/10 08:21
> 亲属社会积极敌视经济增长，因为经济增长破坏了亲属关系的基础：这就是为什么亲属社会要求持续的、明显的财富牺牲——葬礼是最壮观的——这使得任何个人都很难积累资本、再投资他们的资产并取得进步。葬礼是了解财富毁灭体系的一个窗口，最重要的是，它使人们陷入贫困

这种推理是有缺陷的。消费支出不是“财富毁灭”——谁制造了幻想棺材？谁打印横幅？本地企业！

加纳的 GDP 增长率为 5.6%；作为参考，印度的发展成功率为 6.5%。 2000 年加纳的 GDP 为 $5B，今天为 $82.B。同期，其人均GDP增长了一倍多。

>Kinship societies are actively hostile to economic growth, because economic growth undermines the basis of kinship: that is why kinship societies demand constant, visible sacrifices of wealth—funerals being the most spectacular—that make it extraordinarily difficult for any individual to accumulate capital, reinvest their assets, and pull ahead. The funeral is a window into a system of wealth destruction that serves, above all else, to keep people poor

This reasoning is flawed. Consumer spending is not "wealth destruction" -- who makes the fantasy coffins? Who prints the banners? Local businesses!

Ghana is sitting at a 5.6% GDP growth rate; for reference developmental success India is at 6.5%. Ghana's GDP in 2000 was $5B, today it's $82.B. Its per-capita GDP has more than doubled in the same time period.

yongjik 2026/04/10 08:37
另一方面，传统社会中的穷人常常陷入有毒的人际动态中，无法逃脱，因为他们生活在同一个家庭中。

就像在韩国，“婆媳关系问题”曾经非常普遍，以至于有一个词可以形容。 如今，除非您是周末韩剧的粉丝，否则它们越来越难见证。

The flip side of the flip side is that poor people in traditional societies are often trapped in toxic interpersonal dynamics from which there's no escape, because they live in the same household.

Like, in Korea, "mother-in-law vs daughter-in-law relationship issues" used to be so common that there's a single word for that. Nowadays they're getting harder to witness, unless you're a fan of weekend k-dramas.

komali2 2026/04/10 09:22
对经常吹捧的“幸福指数”有很多有效的批评。

不久前我读到的一些内容让我突然想到：芬兰被列为最幸福的国家之一，但处方抗精神病药物和抗抑郁药物的比例也高于正常水平，而且酗酒和自杀的比例也很高。有些东西没有在那里排队。

我自己的轶事经历也有冲突。当我穿越斯堪的纳维亚半岛时，人们看起来……很好。我在那里的朋友说基本上不可以和陌生人说话，每个人都应该安静地互相忽略。与此同时，我越深入越南，甚至深入到人们仍然居住在手工工具搭建的高脚屋里，人们就越快乐、越善于交际。我的朋友们对非洲各国也这么说。

Lots of valid criticisms of the oft touted "happiness index."

Off the top of my head from something I read a while back: Finland is listed as one of the happiest countries, but also has a higher rate than normal of prescribed anti psychotics and anti depressants, and also has high rates of alcoholism and suicide. Something isn't lining up there.

My own anecdotal experience as well conflicts. When I travel through Scandinavia, people seem... Fine. Friends I have there say you're basically not allowed to talk to strangers, at all, everyone is meant to just quietly ignore each other. Meanwhile the deeper I go into Vietnam, even deep into where people still live on stilt houses made by hand tools, the happier and more sociable people are. My friends say the same of various countries in Africa.

孤独和孤独几乎是不同的现象。我在曼哈顿市中心感到最孤独，而在大提顿山脉独自徒步旅行时我感到非常满足。
Loneliness and aloneness are almost separate phenomenon. I've felt my loneliest in the middle of Manhattan and completely fulfilled when on a solo hike in the Grand Tetons.

我其实并不这么认为。我认为如果你对这些结果采取严格的态度，你会发现它们站不住脚。社会学领域有选择性地应用严格性。它的结果很少有真正站得住脚的。

我什至不认为孤独流行是真实的。科学确实没有那么强大。

I don’t actually think so. I think if you apply rigor to those results you find that they don’t stand up. The field of sociology applies rigor selectively. Few of its results actually hold up.

I don’t even think the loneliness epidemic is real. The science is really not that strong.

kakacik 2026/04/10 09:05
这就是争论一方的人喜欢这样想，为自己的决定拍拍自己的背。我已经足够大了，已经看过很多很多次了。

没有简单的胜利——每个人都是不同的，每个家庭都是不同的，一个人蓬勃发展，另一个人则绝对是被囚禁的悲惨时光，没有出路。

世界不是非黑即白的，人也不是，不要这样简单化，因为你错过了现实的样子。

Thats what people on one side of the argument like to think, patting themselves on their back for their own decisions. I am old enough to have seen it many many times.

There is no simple win - each person is different, each family is different, where one thrives the other has absolutely miserable time imprisoned with no way out.

world is not black and white and neither are people, dont dumb it down like that since you miss what reality looks like.

在 HN 中查看
sillysaurusx 2026/04/10 07:21
我希望这是自作自受。相反，它似乎是现代社会的产物。我发布了“如何独处？”稍微探讨一下这个问题：

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47296547 690分，500+评论。

我并不是想博取同情，但如果说这是我自找的，那就错了。我的妻子也没有把它带给我。我们只是随着时间的推移而被侵蚀。但当婚姻纽带被削弱时，他们就会带走家庭纽带。或者至少是她这边的家庭。我的身边不多，所以她的身边是我社交互动的主要来源。

这是一个自我造成的伤害，因为我本可以与我妻子以外的人建立很多社会联系。有时我也尝试这样做。但当你和一个人一起度过 20 年时，你就很难腾出时间来做其他事情，尤其是如果你想做好工作（从研究者的角度来说）。

所以这更像是“二选一：家人、朋友、工作”。我走的是家庭和工作路线。我不后悔，但这意味着现在只剩下工作了，这可能是一种空虚的存在。

幸运的是，现代社会有很多方法可以帮助有动力的人们建立社会纽带。一旦我拿回车，我就会去当地的治疗小组，其中之一是木工治疗小组。与随机的人一起拥有这样的随机爱好听起来很有趣。

要避免的事情似乎是约会应用程序。众所周知，从一种关系跳到另一种关系是一个坏主意。我希望广撒网（参加团体、读书俱乐部、DnD 或其他活动）能够填补这一空白。

老实说，最有帮助的是我有一个女儿。她快三岁了。我很高兴我们拥有了她，只要记住她会过上美好的生活，就能帮助我欣赏自己的生活。

现代社会比以往任何时候都更容易孤立自己。我整天独自坐在房子里，让亚马逊免费提供 USB-C 电缆，而我本周最大的社交互动就是明天上门推销员（讽刺的是，他正试图向我推销一扇门）。这是默认状态；你必须反击它，这很难。但如果说那些随波逐流的人是自残的话，这可能是错误的。社会流动过去是流向社会群体（教会是最明显的例子），而不是最终走向孤独的道路。

I wish it was self inflicted. Instead, it seems to be an artifact of modern society. I posted “How to Be Alone?” exploring this issue somewhat:

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47296547 690 points, 500+ comments.

I’m not trying to get pity, but it would be mistaken to say that I brought it on myself. My wife didn’t bring it on me either. We simply eroded over time. But when marital bonds erode, it turns out they take family bonds with them; or at least, her side of the family. My side isn’t much, so hers was my primary source of social interaction.

This is a self inflicted wound in the sense that I could have formed a lot of social bonds with people other than my wife. And I tried to, sometimes. But when you’re spending 20 years with one person, it’s hard to make time for anything else, especially if you want to do good work (in the researcher sense).

So it’s more of a “pick two: family, friends, work”. I went the family and work route. I don’t regret it, but it means that now all that’s left is work, which can be a hollow existence.

Luckily, modern society has a surplus of ways to help motivated people form social bonds. Once I get my car back, I’ll be going to the local therapy groups, one of which is wood crafting. Random hobbies like that with random people sounds fun.

The thing to avoid seems to be dating apps. Jumping from one relationship into another is universally known as a bad idea. I’m hoping that casting a wide net (going to groups, reading clubs, DnD, or other activities) will fill the void.

Honestly though, what helps the most is that I have a daughter. She’s almost 3. I’m very happy we had her, and just remembering that she’ll have a nice life helps me appreciate my own.

Modern society makes it easier than ever to isolate yourself. I spend my days sitting in a house alone, having Amazon drop off USB-C cables, with my biggest social interaction of the week being the door to door salesman (who, ironically, is trying to sell me a door) that’s coming by tomorrow. That’s the default state; you have to push back against it, and that’s hard. But it’s probably mistaken to say that those who go with the flow are suffering from self inflicted wounds. Societal flow used to be towards social groups (church being the most obvious example) instead of paths that end in loneliness.

kakacik 2026/04/10 09:12
> 选择两个：家人、朋友、工作

这应该总是非常简单，并且永远不会包含工作，除非您可能从事医疗保健或教育工作。给定的教育路径通常会留下很多空闲时间，然后只有前者。

我可能会加上第四个——自己，除非一个人是一个适当的外向者。需要的时间最少，但对长期心理健康最重要。

> pick two: family, friends, work

That should be always supremely easy and never contain work, unless you are maybe working in medical care or education. Given education path normally leaves a lot of free time then just the former.

I would maybe add fourth - oneself, unless one is a proper exreovert. Requires least of the time, but its most important for long term mental health.

在 HN 中查看
stavros 2026/04/10 09:00
在个人主义较少的社会所具有的“家庭中的每个人绝对共享一切”与个人主义较多的社会往往具有的“家庭中的每个人都是孤独的”之间存在着一个折中的办法。

尤其是美国，由于其文化强调工作和自力更生，因此处于这一范围的远端。在我看来，快乐的媒介就是为一些朋友牺牲一些工作。至少在美国文化的许多情况下，你可能会牺牲一定的时间，在工作中获得边际回报，但在友谊中获得更大的回报，仅仅是因为这就是收益递减的原理。

There's a happy medium between the "everyone in the family shares absolutely everything" that less individualistic societies have and the "everyone in the family is alone" that more individualistic societies tend to have.

The US, in particular, is on the far end of that spectrum, because of the cultural emphasis on work and self-reliance. The happy medium, in my opinion, is trading off some work for some friends. In many cases within US culture, at least, you might be trading off an amount of time that yields a marginal reward at work but a much larger reward in friendship, simply because that's how diminishing returns tend to work.

在 HN 中查看
forthwall 2026/04/10 07:34
这篇文章似乎证明，亲属关系导致工资增长和最终财富的失败，人们会隐藏自己的工资，因为人们会要钱。这似乎问题在于，撒哈拉以南非洲的财富积累仅限于一小部分人口，我认为当有一大批人赚更多钱时，家庭成员征收的财富税就不存在。

你可以在美国观察到这一点，想必在世界其他地方，当财富集中到个人身上时，你的家人可能会向你要钱。不同之处在于，收入不平等程度较小，更多的人有能力赚更多的钱。

我确实喜欢对丧葬文化的研究，但我不认为这种亲属关系和家庭关系的假设是缺乏经济流动性的原因。

This article seems to establish that kinship leads to the failure of wage growth and ultimately wealth, people will hide their wages because people will ask for money. This seems like the issue rather is is that wealth accumulation in sub-saharan africa is limited to a small subset of population, I don't think this wealth tax by family members exists when you have a larger group of individuals making more money.

You can observe this in the US, and presumably in the rest of the world, when wealth is concentrated to individuals, your family will probably ask you for money. The difference is here, there is less income inequality and more people have the ability to make more money.

I do like the look into funeral culture, but I don't think this assumption that kinship and family-peity is the cause of the lack of economic mobilty.

在 HN 中查看
themacguffinman 2026/04/10 08:16
不同之处在于，拒绝家庭的金钱要求对你来说是完全可以接受的，它不会让你成为贱民，而且当非家庭机构统治社会时，成为贱民也不会带来同样的后果。

文章花了很多时间阐述这一点：在发达国家你不必做你家人要求你做的事情。另一方面，在一个以血缘关系为主导的社会中，被家人抛弃意味着你无处可去，这会带来巨大的压力。

The difference is that it's pretty acceptable for you to reject family requests for money, it doesn't make you a pariah and being a pariah doesn't carry the same consequences when non-family institutions govern society.

The article spends a lot of time belaboring this point: you don't have to do what your family asks you to do in developed countries. On the other hand, becoming outcast from your family in a kinship-dominated society means you have nowhere else to turn to which is enormous pressure.

在 HN 中查看
ozim 2026/04/10 10:06
在血缘社会里，你无处可去，压力很大。

还有巨大的虐待。

在西方社会，很多人都为失去家庭联系而哀叹。

但他们完全忽视了以下权力带来的滥用行为：

“你没有其他人可以求助，和你的家人在一起，我们会殴打你，抢劫你，但家庭很重要”。

in a kinship-dominated society means you have nowhere else to turn to which is enormous pressure.

Also enormous abuse.

Lots of people lament loss of family ties in western society.

But they completely ignore amount of abuse that comes with power of:

„you have no one else to turn to, stay with your family, we will beat you, rob you, but family is important”.

在 HN 中查看
UltraSane 2026/04/10 10:41
科琳·霍夫曼 (Corinne Hofmann) 是一位瑞士妇女，与肯尼亚桑布鲁战士 Lketinga Leparmorijo 结婚，并育有一女。 她开了一家商店，但他们赔了钱，因为勒马利安给朋友和邻居太多的信用，而且因为他们必须向小酋长行贿。勒马利安认为这没有问题，因为她在瑞士有更多的钱。小酋长要求卡罗拉雇用他十几岁的侄子作为店员。尽管她不需要他，他也不努力，但她必须接受这一点。一段时间后，当他只是喝啤酒而不工作时，她解雇了他。后来他回来攻击她。当地法官裁定，她必须为解雇男孩支付两只山羊的赔偿金，但男孩的家人必须支付她五只山羊的赔偿金，以补偿这次袭击。
Corinne Hofmann is a Swiss woman who married a Kenyan Samburu warrior named Lketinga Leparmorijo and had a daughter. She opens a shop but they lose money because Lemalian gives too much credit to friends and neighbors, and because they have to pay bribes to the mini-chief. Lemalian argues that this is no problem because she has more money in Switzerland. The mini-chief demands that Carola hires his teenage nephew as a shop assistant. She has to accept this although she does not need him and he does not work hard. After some time, when he is just drinking beer and not working, she fires him. Later he returns and attacks her. A local judge rules that she has to pay two goats for firing him, but the boy's family has to pay her five goats to compensate for the attack.
在 HN 中查看
mothballed 2026/04/10 08:56
文章对亲属关系的描述听起来有点像基于家庭的治理和税收。 只有西方政府说，他们的执法者才会高兴地监禁任何不给予政府（“亲属”）所说的欠款的人，而那些拒绝被暴力拖进监狱的人通常会发现命运更糟。

西方版本中，因不付钱而成为贱民是暴力，而不是排斥和羞耻。 当然，除非你足够富有，可以腐败政府本身。

The article's description of kinship sounds a bit like family based governance and taxation. Only with say a western government their enforcers will happily imprison anyone not giving what the government ("kin") says is owed, and those who resist being violently dragged jailed typically find a fate even worse.

The western version then of being a pariah for not paying up is violence rather than ostracization and shame. Of course until you get rich enough that you can corrupt the government itself.

在 HN 中查看
JumpCrisscross 2026/04/10 09:14
> 比如说西方政府，他们的执法者会很高兴地监禁任何不给予政府（“亲属”）所说的应得之物的人

税收和赞助之间的巨大区别在于规则是否已知。你一般都知道 事前 您应该缴纳哪些税款。通过赞助，如果你有意外之财，规则就会调整，要求你捐出它。

> with say a western government their enforcers will happily imprison anyone not giving what the government ("kin") says is owed

Big difference between taxation and patronage is when the rules are known. You generally know ex ante what you should owe in taxes. With patronage, if you have a windfall, the rules adjust to require you give it away.

paulmist 2026/04/10 08:57
我认为作者的观点是财富推动投资，投资推动经济增长。就血缘社会所保证的奢华葬礼而言，财富被用于相对非生产性的投资，而机会成本很高。推论和作者的次要观点是无效的资源分配，例如通过裙带关系。

我从这篇文章中得到的主要（过于简单化！）结论是，亲属关系社会优先考虑抑制全球进程的固有本地进程。例如，他们更喜欢通过仪式庆祝来保持内部凝聚力，而不是通过教育和专业化来最大化经济增长。这是有道理的——后者需要更高程度的信任和稳定性。增加信任度和稳定性似乎是一个渐进的过程。我找到了 Jared Diamond 的《枪炮、病菌与钢铁》[1]，其中给出了一些关于这一点的惊人见解。

[1] https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/1842.Guns_Germs_and_Stee...

I think author's point is that wealth drives investment which drives economic growth. In the case of lavish funerals - warranted in kinship societies - the wealth is spent on relatively unproductive investments bearing high opportunity cost. The corollary and author's secondary point is the ineffective resource allocation e.g. through nepotism.

My main (oversimplified!) takeaway from the article is that kinship societies prioritize inherently local processes that inhibit global processes. For example, they prefer keeping internal cohesion through ritual celebration rather than maximizing economic upside through education and specialization. This makes sense - the latter requires a higher degree of trust and stability. Increasing the degree of trust and stability seems to be an evolutionary process. I found Jared Diamond's Guns, Germs, and Steel [1] to give some amazing insights about this.

[1] https://www.goodreads.com/book/show/1842.Guns_Germs_and_Stee...

在 HN 中查看
AlotOfReading 2026/04/10 10:14
《枪炮、病菌与钢铁》是一本出了名的糟糕的书。

无论如何，看待亲属关系的另一个视角是作为应对不稳定条件的弹性策略。任何给定的压力（干旱、失业等）都不可能对每个人产生同等的影响，因此网络在许多情况下都充当安全网。

如果你仔细观察的话，风险投资在另一个方向上也采用了类似的策略。先验地预测谁会成功是不可能的，因此资本会同时分散到许多不同的赌注上，希望随着时间的推移，成功会超过失败。风险投资生态系统中的许多“仪式”（幽灵招聘、吹嘘、追逐时尚等）对于任何单个公司的成功都不是特别有用，但我认为 HN 上的很多人不会认为这从整体上来说在经济上并不有效。

Guns, Germs, and Steel is a famously awful book.

Anyway, another lens to look at kinship relationships through is as a resilience strategy to volatile conditions. Any given stress (drought, job losses, etc) are unlikely to affect everyone equally, so the network functions as a safety net under many conditions.

Venture capital applies a similar strategy in the other direction if you squint a bit. It's impossible to predict who will succeed a priori, so the capital is spread to many different bets simultaneously in the hope that the successes outweigh the failures over time. Many of the "rituals" in the VC ecosystem (ghost hiring, puffery, fad chasing, etc) aren't particularly useful for any individual company's success, but I don't think many people here on HN are going to argue it's not economically effective as a whole.

在 HN 中查看
wahern 2026/04/10 08:21
加纳的基尼指数仅比美国高几个点（43 比 41），与墨西哥相同。

我认为财富不平等根本无法解释这一点。但任何形式的僵化社会制度往往都会抑制流动性。此外，像这样的亲属群体往往会通过血统来锁定相对财富——一个亲属群体中三代之前最富有的家庭（相对于其他文化）更有可能成为三代之后最富有的家庭。更大的流动性意味着生产力更快地提高，即使整个人口的相对财富差距保持不变，这也会增加每个人的绝对财富。

Ghana's GINI index is only a couple points higher than the US (43 vs 41), and the same as Mexico.

I don't think wealth inequality explains this at all. But what rigid social institutions of any kind tend do is inhibit mobility. Moreover, kinship groups like this tend to lock-in relative wealth by lineage--the wealthiest family of a kin group from 3 generations ago will be much more likely (relative to other cultures) to be the wealthiest family 3 generations from now. Greater mobility means productivity increases faster, which raises absolute wealth for everybody even if relative wealth disparities across the entire population remain constant.

在 HN 中查看
Retric 2026/04/10 08:26
> 即使相对贫富差距保持不变。

相对财富差距随着绝对财富的增加而增加，因为低于最低收入水平人们就会挨饿。也就是说，从长远来看，在自给自足的经济中，你不可能赚到平均工资的 1/10，你就会死。 但一个无家可归的人每月仅靠 500 美元就可以在美国生存数十年。

> even if relative wealth disparities remain constant.

Relative wealth disparity increases as absolute wealth increases because below a minimum level of income people starve. IE you can’t make 1/10th the median wage in a subsistence economy long term you just die. But a homeless person can survive for decades in the US on ~500$ a month.

Does this effect have a name? I wonder how you'd adust for it in a modified GINI metric.
在 HN 中查看
thaumasiotes 2026/04/10 08:45
> 也就是说，从长远来看，在自给自足的经济中，你不可能赚到平均工资的 1/10，你就会死。但一个无家可归的人每月仅靠 500 美元就可以在美国生存数十年。

为此，我想进一步了解两件事：

1. 无家可归者是否在工资中位数明显低于其收入衡量标准的地区生存？ （即“工资中位数的 1/10”是否是通过将外国社区纳入“工资中位数”而产生的幻觉？）

2. 无家可归者的低收入是否通过排除实物施舍（“有人给我买了一个三明治”）和觅食（“我在垃圾箱里发现了一个披萨”）的收入来衡量？

> IE you can’t make 1/10th the median wage in a subsistence economy long term you just die. But a homeless person can survive for decades in the US on ~500$ a month.

There are two things I'd like to know more about for this:

1. Is the homeless person doing their survival in an area with a markedly lower median wage than the median wage their income is being measured against? (i.e. is "1/10 the median wage" an illusion created by including foreign communities in the 'median wage'?)

2. Is the homeless person's low income measured by excluding their income from in-kind handouts ("someone kind bought me a sandwich") and foraging ("I found a pizza in the dumpster")?

在 HN 中查看
forthwall 2026/04/10 08:44
我不认为社会因素不一定是非因素，更何况这篇文章声称亲属群体内的收入平等是经济增长不足的倒逼函数。我的主张是，这些国家不仅在国内相互之间面临不平等，而且在我们的全球化经济中也面临着不平等，资源、资本和劳动力的获取以及市场较小的下游影响，劳动力需求的减少将导致增长放缓。我认为，如果亲属关系中的更多人有更多机会获得财富增长，那么亲属关系社会就能实现经济增长，这里的问题是资源有限，而亲属关系社会的存在是由于资源较少而存在的，反之亦然。

> 3代之前的亲属群体中最富有的家庭（相对于其他文化）更有可能成为3代之后最富有的家庭。

我不确定这种说法是否属实，财富通常确实保留在跨文化的家族血统中，一般来说，失去财富甚至获得财富的人都是异常值。看看欧洲、亚洲的地主贵族

实际上;你可以在美国看到这一点，随着收入继续更加集中、更加不平等，个人的经济生产力确实会下降，因为积累财富的机会比以前少了。

I don't think social factors are not necessarily a nonfactor, more that this article claims that income equality within kinship groups is a forcing function for lack of economic growth. My claim is that the inequality that these countries face not just between each other within the nation but in our globalized economies, access to resources, capital and labor and thus the downstream effects of smaller markets, less need for labor will lead to less growth. I think you can have economic growth with kinship society if more people within the kinship have greater access to wealth growing, the issue here is that there's limited resources and the kinship society exists as an effect of less resources than the other-way around

> the wealthiest family of a kin group from 3 generations ago will be much more likely (relative to other cultures) to be the wealthiest family 3 generations from now.

I am not sure if this claim is true as well, wealth generally does stay within family lineages across cultures, generally people losing their wealth or even gaining it is an outlier. See any landed gentry in Europe, Asia

Actually; you can see this in America, as income continues to be more concentrated, and more unequal, economic productivity for an individual does go down as there's less opportunity to accrue wealth as before.

在 HN 中查看
wahern 2026/04/10 08:58
> 我不确定这种说法是否属实，财富通常确实保留在跨文化的家族血统中，一般来说，失去财富甚至获得财富的人都是异常值。看看欧洲、亚洲的地主贵族

你的例子往往证明了亲属关系结构的影响，这种结构在历史上在所有文化中都更加强大，尤其是西北欧以外的地方（那里的核心家庭动态可以追溯到几千年前，有些人认为这不仅仅是与启蒙运动和工业革命的出现巧合）。

相关问题不在于财富粘性是否存在，而在于 震级 效果及其变化方式。

亲属团体绝对是有用和有益的，但作为一种严格的社会制度，它也可以像任何社会制度一样拥有自己的生命。如果不了解其规模和背景，我们就无法对这些东西进行有意义的讨论，否则很容易愤世嫉俗地模棱两可。

> I am not sure if this claim is true as well, wealth generally does stay within family lineages across cultures, generally people losing their wealth or even gaining it is an outlier. See any landed gentry in Europe, Asia

Your examples tend to prove the effect of kinship structures, which were much stronger historically across all cultures, especially outside NW Europe (where nuclear family dynamics go back millennia, which some people argue is not merely coincidental with the emergence of the Enlightenment and the Industrial Revolution).

The relevant question isn't whether wealth stickiness exists, but the magnitude of the effect and how it changes.

Kinship groups can absolutely be useful and beneficial, but as a rigid social institution it can also take on a life of its own, as for any social institution. We can't have meaningful discussions about this stuff without understanding magnitudes and context, otherwise its too easy to cynically equivocate.

在 HN 中查看
rayiner 2026/04/10 09:36
牢固的延伸亲属关系与世界各地的经济繁荣程度较低有关，这种情况仅在非洲，但在巴基斯坦、中东等地区也是如此。

有一个合理的论点认为这是因果关系。早在中世纪，欧洲就人均国内生产总值开始落后于世界其他国家之前，欧洲的亲属关系就较弱——出于各种原因，包括天主教会禁止表兄弟结婚。即使在美国，亲属关系较弱的社区（例如东北部盎格鲁新教徒）也比亲属关系和氏族结构较强的社区（例如阿巴拉契亚人）在经济上更成功。

可以说，亲属关系较弱的更加原子化的社会促进了民间机构和政府的发展，以补偿原本由亲属关系网络履行的社会结构功能。

Strong extended kinship ties are associated with less economic prosperity all over the world, it just in Africa but Pakistan, the Middle East, etc.

There is a plausible argument that it’s causal. Europe had weaker kinship ties—for various reasons, including the Catholic church’s ban on cousin marriage—back in the middle ages, before Europe began pulling away from the rest of the world in terms of GDP per capita. Even within the U.S., communities with weak kinship ties (e.g. Northeastern Anglo-Protestants) are more economically successful than communities with stronger kinship ties and clan structures (e.g. Appalachians).

Arguably, more atomized societies with weak kinship ties foster the development of civil institutions and governments to compensate for the social structural functions that would otherwise be performed by kinship networks.

> There is a plausible argument that it’s causal. Europe had weaker kinship ties—for various reason

Explain China[1] and its steep ascent, blowing past all European countries, and soon - the USA.

1. Or India, to a lesser extent. There's a lot of recency bias when it comes to economic outcomes, as if we're at the end of history. I'm guessing at least one 19th century British industrialist/gentleman probably praised their Anglo-Saxon heritage and the Protestant (Anglican) faith as necessary ingredients to national wealth, as opposed to the fallen Catholic empires of Spain and Portugal, or the heathens in Africa, the Indo-Pacific, the Middle and far East.

在 HN 中查看
rayiner 2026/04/10 10:35
> 解释一下中国[1]及其急剧上升，超越了所有欧洲国家，很快也超越了美国

共产党打破了传统的家庭结构，取代了与国家的亲属关系。甚至通过独生子女政策对家庭形成本身进行大规模干预。

这与盎格鲁新教本身无关，而是与家庭纽带薄弱的原子化社会的普遍进展有关。多种不同的文化变革都朝着同一个方向推动。新教是其中之一，但在此之前天主教会也是如此： https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/11/roman-catholi...。

> Explain China[1] and its steep ascent, blowing past all European countries, and soon - the USA

The communist party broke down traditional family structures, and replaced kinship ties with the state. To the point of massive intervention in family formation itself, through the one child policy.

It’s not about Anglo-Protestantism per se, but about a general progression towards atomized societies with weak family bonds. Multiple different cultural changes pushed in that same direction. Protestantism was one, but before that so was the Catholic Church: https://news.harvard.edu/gazette/story/2019/11/roman-catholi....

I'm kind of astonished that you think this, there are lots of studies about intergenerational economic mobility in the USA compared to other places.

https://www.chicagofed.org/research/content-areas/mobility/i... for instance.

https://www.mdpi.com/1660-4601/17/4/1179

在 HN 中查看
stavros 2026/04/10 08:48
当然，财富积累仅限于那里的一小部分人口，但在任何地方都是如此。这里的推理错误是从以下角度思考 绝对 整个群体的收入，而不是整个群体的收入 相对的 会员的收入。

是的，美国人平均比加纳人平均赚更多的钱，但是 相对的 一个家庭的收入与加纳人的收入一样悬殊。如果美国有人找到了更好的工作，整个家庭不会突然 还 获得更好的工作。

这就是为什么亲属制度在经济上会产生反作用：集体期望有效地拉低了每个人的水平；任何开始积累财富的个人都面临着在群体中重新分配财富的压力。没有人能够增加自己的财富，因为这需要双方都拥有 一些 最初的财富 和 能够进行使其复合化的投资。如果亲戚群体确保你的财富无法增加，那么你设法做的任何复利都没有关系，因为初始资本总是很小。

Sure, wealth accumulation is limited to a subset of the population there, but this is true everywhere. The reasoning error here is thinking in terms of absolute incomes across the group, rather than the relative incomes of the members.

Yes, people in the US make more money, on average, than the average Ghanaian, but the relative incomes of a family are just as disparate as those of Ghanaians. If someone in the US gets a better job, the whole family doesn't suddenly also get better jobs.

This is why the kinship system is so economically counterproductive: The collective expectation effectively levels everyone down; any individual who begins to accumulate wealth faces pressure to redistribute it across the group. Nobody can grow their fortune, because that requires both having some fortune initially and being able to make investments that compound it. If the kinship group makes sure your fortune can't increase, any compounding you manage to do doesn't matter, because the initial capital always stays small.

在 HN 中查看
komali2 2026/04/10 09:14
似乎这仅在具有复利的经济体系（例如资本主义）中才重要。

在礼品经济中，所有这些区别都是毫无意义的。葬礼的赏金将直接代表死者的受欢迎程度（可能是通过他们的慷慨而建立的）以及他们的家人说服人们的能力。花时间制作漂亮的棺材、带食物、播放音乐。

It seems this only matters in economic systems with compound interest, like capitalism.

In gift economies, all these distinctions are meaningless. The bounty of a funeral would be a direct representation of the deceased's popularity (likely established through their generosity) as well as their family's ability to convice people to e.g. spend time making s beautiful coffin, bring food, play music.

stavros 2026/04/10 09:16
文章中提到的经济体是资本主义经济体。
The economies mentioned in the article are capitalist economies.
在 HN 中查看
komali2 2026/04/10 09:43
是的，国家经济是资本主义的，但家庭团体显然是在某种共产主义下运作的。文章本身就提到了——“你拥有的一切实际上都不是你的，一切都是家人拥有的。”因此，财富积累实际上是不可能的，而这是共产主义经济的标志。

这是我对极端农村地区或紧密结合/亲属/土著文化的经历。他们碰巧生活在一个特定国家的领土上，这个国家不可避免地是资本主义的，因为整个世界都是资本主义的，但仔细观察，你会发现这几乎是村子里一个完全不同的国家。我举一个当地的例子：每个人都认为台湾土著城镇的部落首领是超级富有的地主，因为他们的名字出现在村里的每一块土地和房子上。现实情况：村庄拥有所有共同的东西，当政府官员出现询问谁拥有什么时，村庄并不真正知道如何回答，所以政府官员然后问“好吧，谁是领导者？”然后得到答案，只需记下所有内容的名称即可。

编辑：哦，我明白你的意思，现在，人们正在使用现金来支付，例如DJ。是的，可能是真的，因为这确实让他们花了钱。我的观点是，来自前资本主义体系的、当时运行良好的古老传统可能与资本主义的运作方式并不兼容。很多事情都不是。

Yes, the State economy is capitalist, but the family groups are clearly operating under some kind of Communism. The article itself mentions it - "nothing you own is actually yours, everything is owned by the family." Thus wealth accumulation not really being possible, a hallmark of a communistic economy.

This is my experience with extremely rural areas or tightly knit / kinship / indigenous cultures as well. They happen to live on territory within a given State that's inevitably capitalist because the entire world is, but take a closer look and you'll realize it's almost an entirely different country within the village. A local example to me: everyone assumes the tribal leaders of Taiwanese indigenous towns are super wealthy landowners because their name is on every lot and house in the village. The reality: the villages own everything in common, and when government officials show up asking who owns what, the villages don't really know how to answer, so the government official then asks "ok well who's the leader?" And then getting that answer, just puts that name down for everything.

Edit: oh I see what you mean, right now, people are using cash to pay for e.g. the DJ. Yes, probably true because it's literally costing them money. My point there was that it's possible that an older tradition from a pre capitalist system that worked fine then is not compatible with how capitalism works. Many things aren't.

在 HN 中查看
stavros 2026/04/10 10:03
> 我的观点是，来自前资本主义体系的、当时运行良好的古老传统可能与资本主义的运作方式不兼容。很多事情都不是。

哦，是的，同意了。我可以想象这些社区过去非常孤立，所以什么也没有 到 他们自己知道，这不是他们在村庄周围可以立即看到和触摸到的东西。话又说回来，当时不需要这种仪式性的支出，因此这种支出似乎是对资本主义到达这些社会的直接反应。

> My point there was that it's possible that an older tradition from a pre capitalist system that worked fine then is not compatible with how capitalism works. Many things aren't.

Oh, yes, agreed there. I can imagine that these communities were very insular in the past, so there wasn't really anything to own that wasn't what they could immediately see and touch around the village. Then again, there wouldn't be a need for this ritualistic spending back then, so the spending seems to be a direct reaction to capitalism arriving to these societies.

在 HN 中查看
TheGRS 2026/04/10 07:43
这是一本非常有趣的读物。我很欣赏有人试图深入探究文化的真正原因，而不是仅仅停留在表面价值。我的印象是，从社会学角度来看，这更像是一种可行的理论，而不是事实，因为我确实认为，对于强大的亲属关系网络存在很多反驳的观点，而这些网络原本是富裕和繁荣的。

富裕国家有一个非常明显的相似之处：奢华的婚礼。有很多例子表明，中等收入到低收入的夫妇，即使有家人的支持，也会举办他们实际上负担不起的婚礼，但出于社会习俗，他们还是这么做了。也许这些例子和强大的亲属关系网络之间存在明显的联系。或者也许是因为同侪压力和跟上潮流。

That was a very interesting read. I appreciate when anyone tries to dig into the actual why of culture instead of just leaving it at face value. I get the impression this is more of a working theory than factual on the sociological side, because I do think there's a lot of counter-arguments to be made about strong kinship networks that are otherwise wealthy and prosperous.

And there's a pretty obvious parallel in wealthy nations: the lavish wedding. There are many examples of otherwise modest to low income couples, even with support of their families, putting on weddings they can't really afford but they do it anyway because of social mores. Maybe there's a clear connection between those examples and strong kinship networks. Or maybe its back to peer pressure and keeping up with the joneses.

technothrasher 2026/04/10 07:15
基本上我全家都已经把我们的遗体签字交给了当地的医学院。 他们在接到死亡通知后立即做好一切安排并支付一切费用。 他们通常会在一年左右的时间火葬时给你骨灰，但我个人已经允许他们将我完全骨骼化并无限期保留骨骼。

这通过帮助学生医生学习来帮助社会，并消除所有丧葬麻烦和费用。 不需要尸体，我们还是可以做更多低调的祭奠活动。 我意识到这条道路并不适合所有人，尤其是那些有某些宗教信仰的人，但我们都喜欢这个想法。

Basically my whole family have signed our bodies over to the local medical school. They make all the arrangements and pay for everything as soon as they're notified upon death. They'll normally give you the ashes upon cremation after a year or so, but personally I've given them permission to completely skeletonize me and keep the skeleton indefinitely.

This helps society by helping student doctors learn, and it removes all funeral hassles and expenses. We can still do more low-key memorial ceremonies without needing a body. I realize this path doesn't work for everybody, especially those with certain religious beliefs, but we all just love the idea.

在 HN 中查看
kennyadam 2026/04/10 07:37
我和我的父母都是这样做的。当我妈妈被诊断出患有第四期黑色素瘤时，就不再需要担心安排和寻找资金了。

她从确诊到死亡只用了两个月的时间，所以事情有点令人迷失方向，光是完成一份 RESPECT 表格（又名 DNR）就非常困难，因为与我交谈过的每个人都没有我之前与我交谈过的最后一个人的谈话记录。

当妈妈住进临终关怀医院时，尽管向他们解释了我们所做的安排并向他们展示了文件，但有一天晚上我偶然听到一位护士提到妈妈患有腹水，这是导致她无法捐献遗体的少数原因之一。我用谷歌搜索了一下，意识到我们需要安排并支付她尸体的收集、储存和火化费用。

她第二天早上去世了，幸运的是我能够提前大约两个小时解决这个问题。我想我想表达的观点是，如果您经历了安排将遗体捐献给教学医院的过程（您必须提前自己做），请不要认为它已记录在医疗记录中，也不要认为任何人都会建议您遗体因任何原因不适合捐献。就像英国所有与 NHS 相关的事情一样，系统正在崩溃或崩溃，可怜的员工也是如此，所以你需要为需要它的人辩护，永远不要假设你有一天所说的会传递到下一班。

This is what both my parents and myself did. When my mum was diagnosed with Stage 4 melanoma, it was one less thing to worry about arranging and trying to find money for.

She went from diagnosis to death in two months so things were a bit disorienting and just getting a RESPECT form (aka DNR) completed was such a struggle as everyone I spoke to had no record of my previous conversation with the last person I spoke to.

When mum was admitted to the hospice, despite explaining the arrangements we’d made and showing them the paperwork, it was only by chance that one evening I happened to overhear a nurse mention that mum had ascites, which is one of the few things that disqualified her from being able to donate her body. I googled it and realised we would need to arrange and pay for her body to be collected, stored and cremated.

She died the next morning and luckily I was able to get that sorted about 2 hours earlier. I suppose the point I’m trying to make is that if you go through the process of arranging to donate your body to a teaching hospital (which you must do yourself ahead of time) don’t assume it’s on medical records or that anyone will advise you that the body isn’t suitable for donation for any reason. Like all NHS-related things in the UK, the systems are breaking or broken and so are the poor staff, so you need to advocate for whoever needs it and never assume what you said one day will be passed on to the next shift.

在 HN 中查看
jasonwatkinspdx 2026/04/10 08:12
我只想提一下，当我妈妈的病情开始恶化时，我爸爸聘请了一位医疗保健倡导者并设立了法律机构，这样，当妈妈需要临终关怀的医疗决定或有关葬礼安排的后勤细节时，如果他丧失行为能力，倡导者可以代表妈妈和爸爸之前讨论过的内容，按照他们的意愿。

然后，在我妈妈去世大约一年后，我发现爸爸正在朝那个方向发展，他与同一个倡导者建立了同样的安排。

这是一个伟大的决定。这位倡导者保护了家人免受许多不愉快的细节的影响，使我们能够专注于在父亲的最后几周与他共度美好时光。特别是这对我的姨妈来说是一个巨大的好处，她是我们这个家族中最年长的幸存成员，与我的父亲非常亲密，并且在这种情况下经历了严重的情感动荡。如果没有那位倡导者和父亲事先的愿望非常明确，她就会觉得自己有责任尝试以一种对她来说更加可怕的方式亲自进行他的火葬和纪念活动。

因此，对于即将面临这些情况的任何人，我强烈建议您研究一下这样的事情。有一个不是临终关怀工作人员的第三者，也不是处于情感压力中的亲戚，真是太棒了。父亲之前选择的倡导者是一家疗养院的总经理，后来转而从事倡导工作，作为一种软退休。所以她详细了解这个世界是如何运作的，并且非常擅长为父亲完成工作。

I'll just mention that when my mom was starting to deteriorate, my dad hired a healthcare advocate and set up legal authorities such that if he was incapacitated when mom needed medical decisions in hospice, or logistical details about funeral arrangements, the advocate could represent what mom and dad had discussed prior as their wishes.

Then, a year or so after my mom passed it became clear dad was heading that way, and he set up the same arrangements with the same advocate.

It was a great decision. The advocate shielded the family from a lot of unpleasant details, allowing us to focus on spending as much quality time with dad in his final weeks. In particular it was a huge benefit for my aunt, who's the oldest surviving part of that branch of our family, was very close to my father, and struggled through severe emotional turmoil in the situation. Without that advocate and dad's prior wishes being made very clear, she would have felt duty bound to try to run his cremation and remembrance personally in a way that would have been even more horrific for her.

So for anyone who is facing these situations on the horizon, I strongly suggest looking into something like this. Having a 3rd party that isn't the hospice staff, and that isn't a relative in emotional duress, was fantastic. The advocate dad chose previously was managing director of a care home, and switched to doing advocate work as a sort of soft retirement. So she knew in detail how all of that world works and was excellent at getting stuff done for dad.

在 HN 中查看
squigz 2026/04/10 08:26
这听起来是一项非常有价值的工作。当然，你必须应对许多与死亡相邻的领域所必须承受的悲伤，但至少你会因为真正帮助人们度过那些可怕的时刻而感到满足。

对您的损失深感抱歉。

That sounds like a very rewarding job. Sure, you have to deal with the grief that so many death-adjacent fields have to, but at least you get the satisfaction of really helping people through those terrible times.

So sorry for your losses.

在 HN 中查看
jayknight 2026/04/10 07:28
对于那些确实想埋葬尸体的人来说，它可以比殡葬工业综合体让你相信的便宜得多。我们教堂备有一个简单的棺材，家人可以付费使用。我们有人会准备尸体并将其带到教堂。 我们通常唯一不做的事情就是挖坟墓。墓地通常将其包含在地块成本中。
And for folks that do want to bury the body, it can be done way cheaper than the funeral industrial complex would have you believe. Our church keeps a simple coffin on hand that the family can use at cost. And we have folks who will prepare the body and bring it to the church. The only part we don't usually do is dig the grave. Cemeteries usually include that in the cost of the plot.

Tade0 2026/04/10 07:46
有一部有趣的电影聚焦于这个主题：

https://m.imdb.com/title/tt1499420/

作者走遍了喀麦隆，记录了积压死者必须妥善埋葬的现实情况。

事实证明，并不是每个人都能负担得起将已故亲人放入冰箱的费用 - 尤其是长时间存放，因此有时死者会被存放在房屋旁边的单独存储区中，直到生者筹集到必要的资金为止。

There's an interesting film focused on this topic:

https://m.imdb.com/title/tt1499420/

The author traveled through Cameroon and documented, among other things, the realities of having a backlog of dead one must properly bury.

Turns out not everyone can afford putting their deceased relatives in a freezer - especially for extended periods of time, so sometimes the dead are stored in a separate storage area next to the home until the living gather the necessary funds.

在 HN 中查看
wewtyflakes 2026/04/10 09:19
我发现很多反对亲属关系网络的观点也很容易反对传统的以财富为导向的文化。

“亲属网络有强烈的兴趣阻止其任何成员变得足够繁荣而不再需要它”

看起来就像来自西方的人会感受到的确切的货币压力，只不过它来自老板而不是家庭。最低工资需要作为一种事物而存在就是一个明显的例子。

I found a lot of the points being made against kinship networks are also easily made against traditional wealth-oriented cultures.

"The kinship network has a strong interest in preventing any of its members from becoming prosperous enough to no longer need it"

Seems like the exact monetary pressures someone from the west would feel, except it comes from bosses rather than family. Minimum wage needing to exist as a thing is a clear example of that.

在 HN 中查看
mlsu 2026/04/10 07:22
在美国，我们把这些钱花在婚礼上。许多年轻人在人生最关键的时刻之一（就在组建家庭之前）结婚时耗尽了积蓄。这通常会阻碍他们多年生孩子或买房。
In America we spend that money on weddings. Lots of young people wipe their savings on getting married, at one of the most critical times in life (just before starting a family). It often prevents them having kids or buying a home for years.

不过，把这些钱花在婚礼上比葬礼上要好得多！

squigz 2026/04/10 09:40
> 不过，把这些钱花在婚礼上比葬礼上要好得多！

是吗？您能详细说明一下您为什么这么认为吗？

对我来说，它们似乎具有基本相同的目的。归根结底，它们都是家人和朋友聚在一起并与一个人/一群人建立联系的一种方式。

> Far better to spend those $$ on weddings rather than funerals though !

在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/10 07:43
你总能找到这样的灾难故事：一对夫妇为了一场无力承担的婚礼而花光了积蓄，陷入了财务不稳定的境地，但这实际上是非常常见的。

传统婚礼的费用由父母部分或全部支付。许多富裕的年轻人自己养活自己。如果两者都不是选择，举办小型或本土婚礼也很常见。

如果你认识足够多的人，我们都可能会想到一些过度消费并后悔的人，但我不同意这是常见的文化现象。不过，正义之士喜欢在这个话题上嘲笑其他人这样做。

You can always find disaster stories about couples who wipe out their savings and put themselves in a precarious financial situation for a wedding they can’t afford, but it’s actually super common.

Traditional weddings costs are paid in part or full by the parents. Many well off young people pay their own way. If neither is an option it’s also common to have a smaller or home-grown wedding.

If you know enough people we can all likely think of someone who overspent and regretted it, but I disagree that it’s the common cultural thing to do. It’s a topic where righteous people like to heap scorn on others for doing it, though.

在 HN 中查看
mlsu 2026/04/10 08:18
无论谁出钱，这仍然是非常不合理的。这篇文章在这里探讨的是全社会的财富，不是吗？

我是说聚会？一天就几千块钱？

我做了不理智的事，为我的婚礼付出了很多，这真的很特别。因此，我的家人与她的家人更加亲近。从我和我妻子理性、自利的角度来看，这是没有意义的：如果我们不为婚礼花点钱的话，我们的经济状况会更好。我们的房子可以更大。我们可以更优化地配置我们的资本。

然而，牢固的家庭纽带比这一切更重要。当我们把镜头缩小一点，看看我们的大家庭和朋友群体时，这一切都是有道理的。这些人会帮助我们抚养孩子，在我们运气不好时照顾我们等等。参加婚礼的 50 个人可以因为我们盛大昂贵的婚礼而在彼此的名字上写上面孔。这对我们来说是一个有趣的聚会，但它实际上有一个非常重要的目的。该值不会记录在 GDP 数字中。

我正在嘲笑这篇文章。首先，由于社会压力，我们（开明人士、现代人等）在非理性、毫无意义的事情上花了一大笔钱。我想向这篇文章的作者指出顺势疗法医学，这是一个 10b 的市场；其中十个就相当于加纳的国内生产总值。按 ctrl-f 表示殖民主义、帝国主义或榨取……是的，当然。他们一定很穷，因为他们在葬礼上做了奇怪的事情。

It's still very irrational no matter who pays. The article is looking at a society-wide wealth here isn't it?

I mean a party? Thousands and thousands of dollars for just one day?

I did the irrational thing, paid a lot for my wedding and it was really special. My family is much closer to her family because of it. From me and my wife's rational self-interested perspective, it makes no sense: we would be in a better position financially had we not splurged a little for the wedding. Our house could be bigger. We could have more optimally allocated our capital.

However, the family bonds being strong outweighs all of that. When we zoom out a little bit and look at our extended family and friend group, it all makes sense. These are the people who will help raise our kids, take care of us when we are down on our luck, etc. The 50 people who attended can, because of our big expensive wedding, put faces to each others' names. It was a fun party for us, but it actually served a very important purpose. This value will not be registered in the GDP number.

I'm poking fun at the article. That first of all, we (the enlightened, modern, etc) spend an absolute metric fuckton ton of money on irrational meaningless shit, due to social pressure. I would point the author of the article to homeopathic medicine, which is a 10b market; just ten of these equals the GDP of Ghana. Do a ctrl-f for colonialism or imperialism or extraction and... yeah, sure. They must be poor because they do quirky things at funerals.

Glyptodon 2026/04/10 09:41
我认为人们经常在婚礼上花费太多，但即使是昂贵的婚礼（IMO）仍然与相对无聊和可靠的汽车处于相同的成本范围。如果购买一辆可靠的汽车会让你倒退很多年……我认为这更多地表明收入水平太低，人们正在试图反击“不良耻辱”的信号。 （也就是说，婚礼也是一种从亲属群体中抽出时间可以大大降低成本的事情。在露营地租一个凉亭，让一群人带上烤架和一些扬声器，基本上就可以控制成本。）当然，上流社会的婚礼是完全不同的事情。
I think people often spend too much on weddings, but even expensive weddings (IMO) are still in the same cost bracket as a relatively boring and reliable car. If buying a reliable car sets you back years... I view it more so as a sign that income levels are too low and people are trying to counter signal "poor stigma." (That said, weddings are also the kind of thing where extracting time from your kinship group can drastically lower costs. Rent a pavilion in a camp ground, have a bunch of people bring grills and some speakers and you can basically keep things pretty cost contained.) And of course upper class weddings are a whole different thing.
在 HN 中查看
Thorrez 2026/04/10 08:20
是的，我们在婚礼上花了很多钱，但没有他们在葬礼上花的那么多（根据收入调整）。在加纳，他们在葬礼上的花费是年收入中位数的 2.3 倍至 9 倍[1]。美国的中位收入为 45,140 美元[2]，因此，如果我们在婚礼上的支出与葬礼上的支出相对收入相同，则意味着我们的婚礼费用将为 103,000 美元至 406,000 美元。

[1] https://remotepeople.com/countries/ghana/average-salary/

[2] https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/MEPAINUSA672N

Yes, we spend a lot on weddings, but not as much (adjusted for income) as they do on funerals. In Ghana they spend 2.3x-9x the yearly median income[1] on a funeral. The median income in the US is $45,140[2], so if we were to spend the same amount relative to income on weddings as they do on funerals, that would mean our weddings would be $103k-$406k.

[1] https://remotepeople.com/countries/ghana/average-salary/

[2] https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/MEPAINUSA672N

cortesoft 2026/04/10 07:29

I am so glad we had a big wedding. It was so much fun, and all my friends and family had a blast.
在 HN 中查看
AussieWog93 2026/04/10 07:48
你这么说真是太好笑了。 前几天我实际上只是在和我的妻子谈论婚礼是多么平庸。 你花 20 到 5 万美元基本上是作为有地主的英国绅士来享受生活，最终却比一般的 21 日、圣诞节或新年享受的乐趣更少。 计划方面的压力也更大。
It's so funny you say that. Was literally just chatting to my wife the other day about how mediocre weddings are. You spend $20-$50k basically LARPing as landed British gentry, and end up having less fun than the average 21st, Christmas or New Year's. So much more stress in planning too.
在 HN 中查看
cortesoft 2026/04/10 10:05
我和我的妻子都不是派对人士。除了婚礼之外，我们绝不会出于任何原因举办 100 名朋友和家人的聚会。

看到我所有的朋友和家人在观众席上支持我和我的妻子互相分享我们的誓言，感觉真的很特别。整个仪式我都笑得像个白痴，因为我太高兴了。

我一直很喜欢参加我表弟的婚礼。我家里没有人再有宗教信仰了（我叔叔是一名牧师，但离开了牧师职位，我是无神论者），但我们都非常认真地对待婚姻。我有 10 个表兄弟姐妹和 4 个叔叔阿姨，他们都仍然与他们的第一任配偶结婚。加入那个俱乐部感觉很特别。我是倒数第二个结婚的表弟（我也是倒数第二个结婚的）。

我所有的堂兄弟姐妹也都举办了精彩的婚礼。这些都是大型聚会，我们玩得很开心。我觉得轮到我主持了，感觉很神奇。我们在市中心的图书馆结婚了，这对我们来说是一个特别的地方。我们喜欢带孩子去那里，并向他们展示我们结婚的地方。

花掉这些钱并没有真正改变我的生活。我认为如果我有额外的 6 万美元，事情不会有什么不同。以一辆好车的价格，我们度过了一个永远不会忘记的神奇夜晚，美好的回忆，以及庆祝我们彼此承诺的绝佳方式。这是一生一次的事情。对我来说比一辆好车更有价值。

My wife and I are not party people. We would never host a part with 100 friends and family for any reason other than our wedding.

It felt really special to see all my friends and family out there in the audience supporting my wife and I sharing our vows to each other. I was grinning like an idiot the whole ceremony because I was just so happy.

I had always loved going to my cousin's weddings. No one in my family is religious anymore (my uncle was a priest but left the priesthood, I was raised atheist), but we all do take marriage pretty seriously. I have 10 cousins and 4 sets of aunts and uncles, and all of them are still married to their first spouse. It felt very special to join that club. I was the second to last cousin to get married (I am also the second to youngest).

All my cousins had amazing weddings, too. They were all big parties that we had a lot of fun at. I felt like it was my turn to host one, and it felt magical. We got married at the downtown library, which is a special place for us. We love taking our kids there and showing them where we were married.

Having spent that money hasn't really changed my life in any significant way. I don't think anything would be different if I had an extra $60k. For the price of a nice car, we got a magical night that we will never forget, wonderful memories, and a fantastic way to celebrate our commitment to each other. It was a once in a lifetime thing. Way more valuable to me than a nice car.

在 HN 中查看
Glyptodon 2026/04/10 09:53
我不知道。如果你有 50 个朋友，在一家像样的餐厅预订了位置，并请了一位 DJ，那么即使直到最近在美国大部分地区，你也完全可以享受不到 1 万美元的美好时光。服装+摄影师+戒指加上，但显然根据你的喜好，在这个价格范围内享受真正有趣的时光有很大的自由度。并且有各种各样的替代方案。我们有一些朋友去意大利参加了大约 8 人（家人和亲密朋友）的婚礼，并度过了愉快的时光。我不认为它便宜，但如果一些婚礼派对自己付钱并且他们度过了一个非常有趣的意大利假期，那么它可能低于 2 万美元的低端。我们也有朋友刚刚借了别人的房子，送了一堆食物，然后基本上举办了一场游戏之夜的婚礼。
I don't know. If you had 50 friends, reserved space at a decent restaurant, and got a DJ you could totally have a good time for what's been solidly under $10k even until recently in most of the country. Outfits + photographer + rings add, but there's obviously a lot of latitude to have a really fun time in that price bracket depending on what you like. And there are all kinds of alternatives. We have some friends who went to Italy with a wedding party of about 8 people (family and close friends) and had a great time. I don't think it was cheap, but it was probably below the low end of the $20k if some of the wedding party paid their own way and they had a really fun Italian vacation. We also have friends who just borrowed someone's house, got a pile of food delivered and had basically a game night wedding thing.
在 HN 中查看
cobbzilla 2026/04/10 08:19
我知道你所说的婚礼类型，这令人悲伤，而且很难不同意。

更可悲的是，花 20-5 万美元，你/可以/举办一场超级独特、很棒、甚至低压力的婚礼（好吧，最后一部分取决于父母/亲戚可能是不可能的），但很多都是相同的歌曲（你都知道），相同的场地（庄园、宴会厅、屋顶等），相同的食​​物。

I know the kinds of weddings you speak of, and it’s sad, and hard to disagree.

Even more sad that for $20-$50k you /could/ have a super unique, awesome and even low-stress wedding (ok that last part depending on parents/relatives may be impossible), yet so many are the same songs (you know them all), same venues (estate, banquet hall, rooftop, etc), same food.

cortesoft 2026/04/10 09:56

我们有 100 多人，我们的婚礼在市中心图书馆举行，我们花了大约 6 万美元。这并没有真正影响我们几年后买房的能力，额外的 6 万美元根本不会改变我们的预算。

We do pretty well, although it is expensive living in LA.

We had a little over 100 people, our wedding was at the downtown library, and we spent about $60k. It didn't really hurt our ability to buy a house a few years later, an extra $60k would not have changed our budget at all.

jareklupinski 2026/04/10 07:41
